events.label: reasoning
events.text:
We need answer JSON only. Need diagnose fault: T-tapping on Class B circuit for fire alarm devices & circuits, in-service. NFPA 72 prohibits T-tapping (branching) on Class B circuits? Need recall fire alarm circuits: NFPA 72 defines Class A, B, N etc. Class B circuits use one outgoing and one return path; if single open, devices beyond open don't operate. T-tapping (branching) is generally not permitted on Class B initiating device circuits (IDCs) and notification appliance circuits (NACs) because it creates multiple paths and can compromise supervision? Actually NFPA 72 allows T-taps on Class B? Let's recall. NFPA 72 2019: Class B circuit path must be configured so a single open causes loss of devices beyond. T-taps are not permitted on Class B because they create an untapped branch that can be open without affecting the rest? Let's verify. In fire alarm wiring, Class B allows a single open circuit; devices beyond open are disabled. T-tapping is allowed only on Class A? Hmm.

Class A circuits are looped, return path separate, can operate during single open. T-tapping is generally not allowed on Class A either? Actually Style 6/7 Class A supports T-taps? Let's think.

NFPA 72 2019 Chapter 12: Circuits and Pathways. Class B pathway: "A pathway that has a single connection between the control unit and the device." T-taps are not permitted on Class B. Class A can have T-taps? The code: "Class A and Class B circuits shall be installed so that a single open, short, or ground condition does not affect more than one device/appliance." For Class B, T-tap creates a branch; if branch opens, devices on branch are lost but main remains. But the issue is that a T-tap can cause the circuit to not be properly supervised because an open in the branch may not be detected? Actually if branch is open, the main loop still has continuity, so the open branch is not supervised. That's why prohibited. Class A with return path can detect open branch? Maybe.

The observed field condition: T-tapping on Class B circuit. This is non-compliant. Decision fail. Risk high? Fire alarm circuit compromised supervision; in event of fire, devices beyond T-tap may not operate if branch opens; also inspection failure. Component conditions: installed-defective, non-compliant. Percent complete 100 (in-service). Value 0. Sound_source etc default empty string/0. Workable empty. Findings: T-tapped Class B fire alarm circuit violates NFPA 72 pathway integrity; unsupervised branch can fail without annunciation. Actions: remove T-tap and rewire as continuous Class B home-run or upgrade to Class A as designed; verify with panel supervision test and third-party inspection. Rationale: T-taps on Class B circuits defeat supervision and are prohibited; the branch open would not be detected, leaving devices inoperable. References: NFPA 72 (Class B circuit requirements, pathway integrity). Need exact? Could include "NFPA 72, Chapter 12, Circuits and Pathways, Class B". Since asked apply rather than quote mechanically.

Need ensure JSON valid. No prose outside.

events.text:

  "decision": "fail",
  "risk": "high",

  "component_conditions": ["installed-defective", "non-compliant"],
  "percent_complete": 100,
  "value": 0,
  "sound_source": "",
  "confidence": 0.95,
  "event_time": 0,
  "rate": 0,
  "order": [],
  "workable": [],
  "findings": [
    "T-tap branch installed on a Class B fire alarm circuit",
    "Branch open would not be detected by the panel, defeating circuit supervision",
    "Devices on the T-tap branch could be inoperable during an alarm without any trouble indication"
  ],
  "actions": [
    "Remove the T-tap and rewire the circuit as a continuous Class B home-run to each device",
    "If branching is required, redesign and re-approve the circuit as Class A or other listed supervised pathway",
    "Perform panel supervision and alarm verification tests after correction",
    "Schedule re-inspection with the third-party inspector"
  ],
  "rationale": "NFPA 72 does not permit T-taps on Class B circuits because the branch is not supervised; an open in the branch would not be reported and could leave fire alarm devices non-functional. An in-service system with this wiring is non-compliant and unreliable.",
  "references": [
    "NFPA 72, Chapter 12, Circuits and Pathways, Class B pathway requirements"
  ]
}
